title: Risotto alla milanese
subtitle: Saffron risotto from Milan, golden and deeply savoury.
excerpt: Carnaroli rice toasted in butter, cooked with stock ladle by ladle, finished with saffron, Parmesan and cold butter. The classic of the Milanese table. Serves four as a starter or two as a main.
sourceNotes: Risotto alla milanese. Saffron risotto is documented in northern Italian culinary sources from the 16th century onward. Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, public domain) contains foundational risotto technique. The dish is traditionally served as a side with osso buco (risotto alla milanese con osso buco).

content.text: Risotto is not difficult but it requires attention. The technique is not complicated: toast the rice, add wine, add stock gradually, finish with butter and cheese. What separates a good risotto from a mediocre one is the mantecatura, the final, vigorous beating of cold butter and Parmesan into the rice off the heat. This is what creates the flowing, creamy texture.
The saffron is not optional here. It is the dish. Bloom it in warm stock first; this extracts more colour and flavour than adding the threads directly to the rice.

items.name: Saffron
items.prepNote: threads, bloomed in 2 tbsp warm stock

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: 30 g for cooking, 30 g cold for the mantecatura

content.text: Bloom the saffron and heat the stock
Steep {{saffron}} of saffron threads in 2 tablespoons of warm stock for 10 minutes. Keep the remaining {{stock-chicken}} of stock in a pan over low heat; it should be hot when you add it to the rice.

Melt 30 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large, wide pan over medium heat. Add {{onion}} diced onion and cook for 8 minutes until very soft but not coloured. Add {{carnaroli-rice}} of rice and stir for 2 minutes until the grains are coated and slightly translucent at the edges. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine; stir until absorbed.

Add the hot stock, one ladle at a time, stirring after each addition until almost absorbed before adding the next. After 10 minutes, stir in the saffron and its soaking liquid. Continue adding stock and stirring for another 8 to 10 minutes until the rice is cooked but still has a very slight bite. You may not need all the stock.

Take the pan off the heat. Add the remaining cold 30 g of butter, cut into small pieces, and {{parmesan}} of grated Parmesan. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon, rocking the pan, for 1 to 2 minutes until the risotto is creamy and flows like lava. Cover the pan and leave to rest for 2 minutes. Serve immediately in warm bowls.

items.fix: Risotto should flow. Add a splash of hot stock and more butter; beat vigorously. If it has cooled, it will set, risotto must be served the moment it is ready.
items.cause: Overcooked, or not enough butter at the mantecatura stage.

items.fix: Always bloom saffron in warm liquid for at least 10 minutes. Adding dry threads directly to the rice wastes most of their flavour.
items.cause: Saffron not bloomed, or threads not steeped long enough.

items.fix: Add stock in small ladles, one at a time. Taste regularly from 15 minutes; the rice should yield but not be chalky.
items.cause: Not enough stock, or added too fast without allowing absorption.

content.text: The golden risotto of Milan is traditionally the side dish to osso buco, the braised veal shank that is the city's other great dish. Together they make one of the classic pairings in Italian cooking: the rich, gelatinous veal with the saffron rice. Either dish is worth making alone, but eaten together they belong to a different register entirely.
